---
output: github_document
---

<!-- README.md is generated from README.Rmd. Please edit that file -->

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  fig.path = "man/figures/README-",
  out.width = "100%"
)
```

# rDataSet

<!-- badges: start -->
<!-- badges: end -->

The goal of rDataSet is to treat data frames as mathematical sets. It provides set-theoretic operations (union, intersection, difference, equality) for data frames where values are identified by row IDs and column names, and presence/absence is determined by `NA` values.

Empty, rows and empty cols are dropped. More over, redundant id cols are dropped, meaning if all values are constant over an id. that id is dropped and the set is shrunk.

When a dataset is in this reduced / restricted format, many operations become possible which wouldn't be reasonable without those restrictions.

Mainly we can convert the Datasets between, wide (default), long, decomposed, flattened states. Where each represents a structure which is ideal for certain operations.

- wide: is the standard, csv / excel view
- long: is really neat for filtering and diff views.
- decomposed: really neat to extract "metadata" from datasets. Data which strictly correlates with one id, instead of all.
- flat: is usefull for serializing and storing data as text in a human readable way.


## Installation

You can install the development version of rDataSet from local source like so:

``` r
devtools::install("/path/to/rDataSet")
# or with
install.packages("/path/to/rDataSet", repo = NULL, type = "source")
```

## Overview

rDataSet introduces a `dataset` S3 class that extends data frames with designated ID columns. Set operations work on a cell-by-cell basis:

- **ID columns** uniquely identify each row (one-to-one relation)
- **Value presence** is determined by `is.na()` - a value is present if not `NA`
- **Set operations** compare cells at the same (row_id, col_id) position
- **Empty rows and cols are dropped** - rows and cols containing only `NA` values in all value columns are considered non-existent and are automatically removed during dataset construction
- **Redundant Ids are dropped** - Ids which have no variance in their data are dropped. 

## Set Operations

Each set operation works in two stages:

1. **Row/Column Alignment**: Determines which rows and columns appear in the result
2. **Value Operation**: Decides which values to keep for each cell

| Operation | Rows in Result | Columns in Result | Value Operation |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Difference (`A - B`) | All rows from A | All value columns from A | Keep values from A where B has `NA` |
| Intersection (`A > B`) | Only rows present in **both** A and B (inner join on IDs) | Only common value columns | Keep values from A where B has non-`NA` |
| Union (`A + B`) | All rows from both A and B (full join on IDs) | All value columns from both | Use A's value; fall back to B where A has `NA` |
| Equality (`A == B`) | Must match exactly (same rows) | Must match exactly (same columns) | Must match exactly (same value) |

**Note**: ID columns must match between datasets for all operations. Value columns that exist in only one dataset are handled according to the join type (dropped, kept, or error).

## License

GPL (>= 3)
